## Topological Insulators

Topological insulator materials are a recently discovered electronic phase with unique
properties: such as spin-polarized protected surface/edge states akin to gapless Dirac states
in Graphene and bulk magneto-electric effects, amongst others. TI materials have promising applications in spin based memory & logic and magnetoelectric switches.
Our research on TI materials focuses on exploring the nature of charge and spin transport in TI devices and high quality material synthesis.
Current topics include:

* Growth of high-quality thin films of TI materials in MBE and vdWE
* Magnetoransport experiments in TI devices
* TI based magnetoelectronics and spin-based switching devices
* Charge & spin transport physics and electronic structure

--- SOURCE 3: Topological Materials - Shen Laboratory - Stanford University ---
URL: https://arpes.stanford.edu/research/quantum-materials/topological-materials

SUMMARY:
Topological insulators are a newly discovered state of quantum matter. They feature a bulk gap and an odd number of relativistic Dirac fermions on their surfaces . While their bulk is insulating, the surfaces can conduct electric current with a well-defined spin texture. Spin-orbit interaction plays an important role.

FULL CONTENT:
# Topological Materials

## Main navigation

**Topological insulators** are a newly discovered state of quantum matter. They feature a bulk gap and an odd number of relativistic [Dirac fermions](https://shenlab.sites.stanford.edu/research/quantum-materials/topological-materials#f4) on their surfaces. While their bulk is insulating, the surfaces can conduct electric current with a well-defined [spin texture](https://shenlab.sites.stanford.edu/research/quantum-materials/topological-materials#f3). Spin-orbit interaction plays an important role. As a result, these materials are classified differently from the traditional Landau theory of phase transitions in matter. Instead, they are categorized by a topological quantity in the electron's wave-function, the *Chern number*. In addition, the *linear energy-momentum relationship* (*E* = *pv*) of electrons in these materials also appears in relativity (*E* = *pc*). As the velocity (*v*) of massless particles here are about 200 times slower than the speed of light (*c*) in vacuum, they are a great way to study the physics of relativity manifest in a condensed matter system.

![](/sites/g/files/sbiybj26276/files/styles/responsive_large/public/media/image/research_ti1_0.png?itok=7KlEGIWh)
`2`
`3`
`2`
`3`

Unlike non-topological ('trivial') materials, where fragile surface states can be easily altered by imperfections in surface geometry and chemistry, topological insulators are predicted to have unusually robust surface states protected by **time-reversal symmetry**. These unique states are protected against all time-reversal-invariant perturbations, such as scattering by non-magnetic impurities, crystalline defects, and distortion of the surface itself. This can produce striking quantum phenomena such as quantum spin Hall [(QSH)](https://shenlab.sites.stanford.edu/research/quantum-materials/topological-materials#f6) and quantum anomalous Hall (QAH) effects, image magnetic monopoles induced by electric charges, and Majorana fermions (which are their own anti-particle — expected for bosons but not fermions) induced by proximity to a superconductor.

Extracting the electronic and structural properties of topological insulators is essential for both understanding the underlying physics and potential applications. As ARPES is a direct method to study the electron band structures of solids, it obtains [detailed information on the electronic bands](https://shenlab.sites.stanford.edu/research/quantum-materials/topological-materials#f1) of topological insulators, and can even demonstrate control over the electronic surface states of topological insulators in the [time domain](shenlab.sites.stanford.edu/research/tool-development/time-resolved-arpes#Result3). ARPES has emerged as the leading tool to elucidate the topological nature of 3D topological insulators.

##### [Electronic structure of the 3D topological Insulator Bi2Te3](#f1)

##### [Probing the unoccupied states in topological insulator Bi2Se3](#f2)

##### [Spin-resolved imaging of the surface state of topological insulator Bi2Se3](#f3)

##### [Massive Dirac fermion on the surface of the magnetically doped topological insulator Bi2Se3](#f4)

##### [Discovery of the topological semimetal Na3Bi](#f5)

##### [Quantum Spin Hall Insulator WTe2](#f6)

## Electronic structure of the 3D topological Insulator Bi2Te3

![](/sites/g/files/sbiybj26276/files/styles/responsive_large/public/media/image/research_ti2_0.png?itok=qvmgLkei)
`2`
`3`
`2`
`3`

Bi2Te3 has been proposed to be the simplest 3D topological insulator, with a surface state consisting of a **single Dirac cone** at the Γ point (***k*** = 0 in the Brillouin zone). By scanning across the Brillouin zone, our ARPES results on Bi2Te3 demonstrate that the surface state consists of a single non-degenerate Dirac cone. Furthermore, we explicitly showed the existence of a 100 meV energy gap for the bulk state by tuning the Fermi level. Our results demonstrate that Bi2Te3 is a possible candidate for high-temperature spintronic applications.

## Probing unoccupied states in the topological insulator Bi2Se3

Just like [Bi2Te3](https://shenlab.sites.stanford.edu/research/quantum-materials/topological-materials#f1), Bi2Se3 is also predicted to be a near-ideal topological insulator with a single Dirac cone. Bi2Se3 offers the potential for topologically protected behavior in ordinary crystals at room temperature and zero magnetic field. It has a large band gap of 0.3 eV at 360 K, which is promising for spintronic applications. For topological insulators, understanding the interplay of **spin-polarized surface electrons** with non-spin-polarized bulk electrons is critical for these potential applications. Our work reveals the coupling between bulk and surface electrons of the topological insulator Bi2Se3 by probing the dynamics of optically excited electrons directly in the electronic band structure using time-resolved ARPES [(trARPES)](https://shenlab.sites.stanford.edu/research/tool-development/time-resolved-arpes).

![](/sites/g/files/sbiybj26276/files/styles/responsive_large/public/media/image/research_bi2se3_0.gif?itok=0tlNXPc6)

This femtosecond 'movie' on Bi2Se3 depicts how excited electrons fill unoccupied bands, and are subsequently prevented from relaxing immediately back to equilibrium due to the bulk band gap. These hot electrons accumulate at the bulk conduction band edge and act as a reservoir, which continues to populate the spin-polarized surface states for more than 10 picoseconds. This finding may pave the way for development of ultrafast optical switches of spin-polarized conduction channels. By resonantly exciting electrons in Bi2Se3 to unoccupied states, we obtain a complete picture of the electronic structure from the Fermi level up to the vacuum level. We found that the unoccupied states host a second, topologically protected Dirac surface state which can be resonantly excited by 1.5 eV photons.

![](/sites/g/files/sbiybj26276/files/styles/responsive_large/public/media/image/photocurrent_0.png?itok=jWnbK24V)
`2`
`3`

In addition, our knowledge of the unoccupied band structure was instrumental in our investigation of **photocurrents** generated via circularly polarized optical excitations in Bi2Se3. These photocurrents can be measured in conventional transport configurations and are carried by topological surface states. Light helicity controls the photocurrent direction, making such systems interesting for electronic applications. The spectroscopic signature of photocurrents in trARPES is an **asymmetric electron population** in momentum space. We found that in Bi2Se3, only resonant optical transitions lead to momentum-asymmetric electron populations and therefore contribute to photocurrent generation. We also observed that different bands contribute to the net current in opposite directions. Our work provides a microscopic understanding of how to control photocurrents in topological materials and sets the stage for further studies leading toward spintronic applications.

## Spin-resolved imaging of the surface state of topological insulator Bi2Se3

![](/sites/g/files/sbiybj26276/files/styles/large/public/media/image/bite_1.png?itok=AUqEYfyt)

**Spin-orbital texture** in topological surface states is a special characteristic of topological insulators in which spin direction is locked to the wave vector and winds twice around the Fermi surface. Probing spin texture in topological insulators is thus crucial for understanding them. Our newly-developed [spin-ARPES](https://shenlab.sites.stanford.edu/research/tool-development/spin-arpes) technique has the ability to probe spin texture in electronic band structure. The figure above shows spin-polarized surface states we have measured in Bi2Se3. Red and blue colors indicate opposite in-plane spin polarization. The spin-momentum locking feature of the topological surface state is clearly shown, where opposite momenta possess opposite spin polarizations.

## Massive Dirac fermion on the surface of the magnetically doped topological insulator Bi2Se3

![](/sites/g/files/sbiybj26276/files/styles/responsive_large/public/media/image/mn-bi2se3_0.png?itok=NxNfZix0)
`2`
`3`

Topological insulators accommodate a conducting, linearly dispersed **Dirac surface state** in addition to the bulk energy gap. This state is predicted to become massive — that is, they form a gap and lose their linear dispersion — if time reversal symmetry is broken. It is also insulating if the Fermi energy (*E*F) is positioned inside both the surface and bulk gaps. This is shown in the diagram above. We can force an insulating massive **Dirac fermion state** to form by tuning *E*F into the surface-state gap. This state may then support many striking topological phenomena, such as an image magnetic monopole induced by a point charge, the half quantum Hall effect on the surface, and a topological contribution to the Faraday and Kerr effects.

We introduced long-range magnetic order to the 3D topological insulator Bi2Se3, breaking time-reversal symmetry by adding magnetic dopants. We positioned the *E*F inside the gaps by simultaneous magnetic and charge doping. Using ARPES, we studied the electronic structures of undoped and doped Bi2Se3. For undoped Bi2Se3, *E*F was found to always reside above the Dirac fermion state. Introducing Mn dopants to Bi2Se3 lifted the degeneracy at the Dirac point, forming an approximately 7 meV time-reversal symmetry-broken gap. It also tuned the *E*F into this gap, removing excess *n*-type carriers while maintaining the magnetic doping effect. In other words, Mn dopants not only introduce magnetic moments into the system, but also naturally *p*-dope the samples. The resulting insulating massive Dirac fermion state we discovered paves the way for studying a range of topological phenomena relevant to both condensed matter and particle physics.

## Discovery of the topological semimetal Na3Bi

![](/sites/g/files/sbiybj26276/files/styles/responsive_large/public/media/image/na3bi_0.png?itok=N7MbzVGV)
`3`

In contrast to 2D Dirac fermions in graphene, or the *surfaces* of 3D topological insulators, **topological Dirac semimetals** (TDS) possess 3D Dirac fermions in the *bulk* and can be viewed as a 3D counterpart of graphene. The bulk conduction and valence bands touch only at Dirac points and disperse linearly along momentum directions, forming 3D Dirac fermions in the bulk. The TDS state is proximate to various quantum states, ranging from regular band insulators to topological superconductors, and is thus useful for studying topological phase transitions.

We have performed angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements to investigate the electronic structures of Na3Bi single crystals, and validated its description as a 3D counterpart of graphene. Our data clearly observed the Dirac cone in Na3Bi and showed that it is 3D (bulk) in nature. We also found that the bulk Dirac cone persists despite surface deterioration, supporting the notion that the Dirac fermion observed is protected by the bulk crystal symmetry. The discovery of the topological Dirac semimetal Na3Bi opens the door to exploring other 3D TDSs and is potentially useful for spintronic applications.

## Quantum Spin Hall Insulator WTe2

![](/sites/g/files/sbiybj26276/files/styles/responsive_large/public/media/image/wte2_1_0.png?itok=jCLD3FGu)
`2`
`2`

A **quantum spin Hall** insulator (QSH), or a two-dimensional topological insulator, possesses an insulating bulk, and topologically protected dissipationless edge states that bridge the energy gap opened by band inversion and strong spin-orbit coupling. A QSH state features quantized Hall conductance in the absence of a magnetic field and is thus promising for spintronic applications.

In our study, we demonstrated successful growth of monolayer 1T’ phase of WTe2 on a bilayer graphene substrate using molecular beam epitaxy [(MBE)](https://shenlab.sites.stanford.edu/research/tool-development/molecular-beam-epitaxy). Using ARPES and STS (scanning tunneling spectroscopy), we established that monolayer 1T’-WTe2 is a new class of QSH insulator, with nontrivial band inversion, opening of a 55 meV bulk band gap, and a conducting edge state coexistent with an insulating bulk. This finding provides a platform for studying QSH insulators in 2D transition metal dichalcogenides, and for developing novel device applications. More information on thin films and 2D materials may be found [here](/research/quantum-materials/thin-films-interfaces-and-2d-materials "Thin films, Interfaces and 2D Materials").

## Address

McCullough Building 342

Stanford, CA 94305

--- SOURCE 4: Topological insulator - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topological_insulator

SUMMARY:
A topological insulator is a material whose interior behaves as an electrical insulator while its surface behaves as an electrical conductor.

FULL CONTENT:

## Contents

# Topological insulator

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **reads like a scientific review article and potentially contains [biased](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") [syntheses](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Synthesis_of_published_material "Wikipedia:No original research") of [primary sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research").** Please replace inadequate primary references with secondary sources. See the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Topological_insulator#Review "Talk:Topological insulator") for details. *(February 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Ambox_question.svg/40px-Ambox_question.svg.png)![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Topological_insulator_informal_phase_diagram-en.svg/250px-Topological_insulator_informal_phase_diagram-en.svg.png)
![{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} _{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/92aedfb5c02eff978ab963421ce930f46801657e)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Topological_insulator_band_structure.svg/250px-Topological_insulator_band_structure.svg.png)

A **topological insulator** is a material whose interior behaves as an [electrical insulator](/wiki/Electrical_insulator "Electrical insulator") while its surface behaves as an [electrical conductor](/wiki/Electrical_conductivity "Electrical conductivity"),[[3]](#cite_note-KM-3) meaning that electrons can only move along the surface of the material.

A topological insulator is an insulator for the same reason a "[trivial](/wiki/Triviality_(mathematics) "Triviality (mathematics)")" (ordinary) insulator is: there exists an [energy gap](/wiki/Band_gap "Band gap") between the [valence and conduction bands](/wiki/Valence_and_conduction_bands "Valence and conduction bands") of the material. But in a topological insulator, these bands are, in an informal sense, "twisted", relative to a trivial insulator.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The topological insulator cannot be [continuously](/wiki/Continuous_function "Continuous function") transformed into a trivial one without untwisting the bands, which closes the band gap and creates a conducting state. Thus, due to the continuity of the underlying field, the [border](/wiki/Surface_state "Surface state") of a topological insulator with a trivial insulator (including [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum"), which is topologically trivial) is forced to support conducting [edge states](/wiki/Edge_states "Edge states").[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5)

Since this results from a global property of the topological insulator's [band structure](/wiki/Band_structure "Band structure"), local (symmetry-preserving) perturbations cannot damage this surface state.[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6) This is unique to topological insulators: while ordinary insulators can also support conductive surface states, only the surface states of topological insulators have this robustness property.

This leads to a more formal definition of a topological insulator: an insulator which cannot be [adiabatically](/wiki/Adiabatic_theorem "Adiabatic theorem") transformed into an ordinary insulator without passing through an intermediate conducting state.[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5) In other words, topological insulators and trivial insulators are separate regions in the [phase diagram](/wiki/Phase_diagram "Phase diagram"), connected only by conducting phases. In this way, topological insulators provide an example of a [state of matter](/wiki/State_of_matter "State of matter") not described by the [Landau symmetry-breaking theory](/wiki/Landau_theory "Landau theory") that defines ordinary states of matter.[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6)

The properties of topological insulators and their surface states are highly dependent on both the dimension of the material and its underlying [symmetries](/wiki/Symmetry_in_quantum_mechanics "Symmetry in quantum mechanics"), and can be classified using the so-called [periodic table of topological insulators](/wiki/Periodic_table_of_topological_invariants "Periodic table of topological invariants"). Some combinations of dimension and symmetries forbid topological insulators completely.[[7]](#cite_note-:12-7) All topological insulators have at least [U(1) symmetry](/wiki/U(1) "U(1)") from [particle number](/wiki/Particle_number "Particle number") conservation, and often have [time-reversal symmetry](/wiki/Time-reversal_symmetry "Time-reversal symmetry") from the absence of a magnetic field. In this way, topological insulators are an example of [symmetry-protected topological order](/wiki/Symmetry-protected_topological_order "Symmetry-protected topological order").[[8]](#cite_note-8) So-called "topological invariants", taking values in 





Z

2
{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} \_{2}}
![{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} _{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/92aedfb5c02eff978ab963421ce930f46801657e) or 




Z
{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} }
![{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/449494a083e0a1fda2b61c62b2f09b6bee4633dc), allow classification of insulators as trivial or topological, and can be computed by various methods.[[7]](#cite_note-:12-7)

![{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} _{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/92aedfb5c02eff978ab963421ce930f46801657e)
![{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/449494a083e0a1fda2b61c62b2f09b6bee4633dc)

The surface states of topological insulators can have exotic properties. For example, in time-reversal symmetric 3D topological insulators, surface states have their [spin](/wiki/Spin_quantum_number "Spin quantum number") locked at a right-angle to their momentum (spin-momentum locking). At a given energy the only other available electronic states have different spin, so ["U"-turn scattering](/wiki/Umklapp_scattering "Umklapp scattering") is strongly suppressed and conduction on the surface is highly metallic.

Despite their origin in quantum mechanical systems, analogues of topological insulators can also be found in classical media. There exist [photonic](/wiki/Photonic_topological_insulator "Photonic topological insulator"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) [magnetic](/wiki/Magnetic_topological_insulators "Magnetic topological insulators"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) and [acoustic](/wiki/Acoustics "Acoustics")[[11]](#cite_note-11) topological insulators, among others.

## Prediction

The first models of 3D topological insulators were proposed by B. A. Volkov and O. A. Pankratov in 1985,[[12]](#cite_note-Volkov85-12)
and subsequently by Pankratov, S. V. Pakhomov, and Volkov in 1987.[[13]](#cite_note-Pankratov87-13) Gapless 2D Dirac states were shown to exist at the band inversion contact in [PbTe](/wiki/PbTe "PbTe")/[SnTe](/wiki/SnTe "SnTe")[[12]](#cite_note-Volkov85-12) and [HgTe](/wiki/HgTe "HgTe")/[CdTe](/wiki/CdTe "CdTe")[[13]](#cite_note-Pankratov87-13) heterostructures.
Existence of interface Dirac states in HgTe/CdTe was experimentally verified by [Laurens W. Molenkamp's](/wiki/Laurens_W._Molenkamp "Laurens W. Molenkamp") group in 2D topological insulators in 2007.[[14]](#cite_note-König07-14)

Later sets of theoretical models for the 2D topological insulator (also known as the quantum spin Hall insulators) were proposed by [Charles L. Kane](/wiki/Charles_L._Kane "Charles L. Kane") and [Eugene J. Mele](/wiki/Eugene_J._Mele "Eugene J. Mele") in 2005,[[15]](#cite_note-15) and also by [B. Andrei Bernevig](/wiki/B._Andrei_Bernevig "B. Andrei Bernevig") and [Shoucheng Zhang](/wiki/Shoucheng_Zhang "Shoucheng Zhang") in 2006.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The 





Z

2
{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} \_{2}}
![{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} _{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/92aedfb5c02eff978ab963421ce930f46801657e) topological invariant was constructed and the importance of the time reversal symmetry was clarified in the work by Kane and Mele.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Subsequently, Bernevig, Taylor L. Hughes and Zhang made a theoretical prediction that 2D topological insulator with one-dimensional (1D) helical edge states would be realized in quantum wells (very thin layers) of mercury telluride sandwiched between cadmium telluride.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The transport due to 1D helical edge states was indeed observed in the experiments by Molenkamp's group in 2007.[[14]](#cite_note-König07-14)

![{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} _{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/92aedfb5c02eff978ab963421ce930f46801657e)

Although the topological classification and the importance of time-reversal symmetry was pointed in the 2000s, all the necessary ingredients and physics of topological insulators were already understood in the works from the 1980s.

In 2007, it was predicted that 3D topological insulators might be found in binary compounds involving [bismuth](/wiki/Bismuth "Bismuth"),[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) and in particular "strong topological insulators" exist that cannot be reduced to multiple copies of the [quantum spin Hall state](/wiki/Quantum_spin_Hall_effect "Quantum spin Hall effect").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Properties and applications

Spin-momentum locking[[24]](#cite_note-Hsieh09-24) in the topological insulator allows symmetry-protected surface states to host [Majorana particles](/wiki/Majorana_particle "Majorana particle") if superconductivity is induced on the surface of 3D topological insulators via proximity effects.[[25]](#cite_note-25) (Note that Majorana zero-mode can also appear without topological insulators.[[26]](#cite_note-Potter-26)) The non-trivialness of topological insulators is encoded in the existence of a gas of [helical Dirac fermions](/wiki/Helical_Dirac_fermion "Helical Dirac fermion"). Dirac particles which behave like massless relativistic fermions have been observed in 3D topological insulators. Note that the gapless surface states of topological insulators differ from those in the [quantum Hall effect](/wiki/Quantum_Hall_effect "Quantum Hall effect"): the gapless surface states of topological insulators are symmetry-protected (i.e., not topological), while the gapless surface states in quantum Hall effect are topological (i.e., robust against any local perturbations that can break all the symmetries). ![{\displaystyle \mathbb {Z} _{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/92aedfb5c02eff978ab963421ce930f46801657e)

The most promising applications of topological insulators are spintronic devices and dissipationless [transistors](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor") for [quantum computers](/wiki/Quantum_computer "Quantum computer") based on the [quantum Hall effect](/wiki/Quantum_spin_Hall_effect "Quantum spin Hall effect")[[14]](#cite_note-König07-14) and [quantum anomalous Hall effect](/wiki/Quantum_anomalous_Hall_effect "Quantum anomalous Hall effect").[[31]](#cite_note-31) In addition, topological insulator materials have also found practical applications in advanced [magnetoelectronic](/wiki/Magnetoelectronics "Magnetoelectronics") and [optoelectronic](/wiki/Optoelectronic "Optoelectronic") devices.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Thermoelectrics

Some of the most well-known topological insulators are also [thermoelectric materials](/wiki/Thermoelectric_materials "Thermoelectric materials"), such as Bi2Te3 and its alloys with Bi2Se3 (n-type thermoelectrics) and Sb2Te3 (p-type thermoelectrics).[[34]](#cite_note-34) High thermoelectric power conversion efficiency is realized in materials with low thermal conductivity, high electrical conductivity, and high [Seebeck coefficient](/wiki/Seebeck_coefficient "Seebeck coefficient") (i.e., the incremental change in voltage due to an incremental change in temperature). Topological insulators are often composed of heavy atoms, which tends to lower thermal conductivity and are therefore beneficial for thermoelectrics. A recent study also showed that good electrical characteristics (i.e., high electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient) can arise in topological insulators due to warping of the bulk band structure, which is driven by band inversion.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Often, the electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient are conflicting properties of thermoelectrics and difficult to optimize simultaneously. Band warping, induced by band inversion in a topological insulator, can mediate the two properties by reducing the effective mass of electrons/holes and increasing the valley degeneracy (i.e., the number of electronic bands that are contributing to charge transport). As a result, topological insulators are generally interesting candidates for thermoelectric applications.

## Theoretical background

### Topology of the Brillouin zone

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Ebene_zu_Torus_verkleben_01.png/330px-Ebene_zu_Torus_verkleben_01.png)

A periodic system is, by definition, invariant under some translations, allowing us to label the [eigenstates](/wiki/Quantum_state "Quantum state") of a periodic [Hamiltonian](/wiki/Hamiltonian_(quantum_mechanics) "Hamiltonian (quantum mechanics)") by the eigenvalues of the [translation operators](/wiki/Translation_operator_(quantum_mechanics) "Translation operator (quantum mechanics)") that leave the system unchanged. The eigenvalues of the translation operator are called [crystal momenta](/wiki/Crystal_momentum "Crystal momentum") (or quasimomenta) and are usually written as

![{\displaystyle \mathbf {k} =(k_{1},k_{2},...,k_{d})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/14f53dee7c2429a178b14db56e8ddcee06880709) (where d is the number of dimensions of the system). Those [quantum numbers](/wiki/Quantum_number "Quantum number") play a role analogous to the [wavevector](/wiki/Wave_vector "Wave vector") of a [free particle](/wiki/Free_particle_(quantum_physics) "Free particle (quantum physics)"). The periodicity of the crystal induces a periodicity in k-space:

The set of all quasimomenta that are not equivalent up to a reciprocal wavevector is called the [Brillouin zone](/wiki/Brillouin_zone "Brillouin zone").[[36]](#cite_note-36) As a consequence of the periodicity of the Brillouin zone, its opposite edges or faces should be identified with each other, therefore the Brillouin zone describes a d-dimensional [torus](/wiki/Torus "Torus"),

### Two-band Hamiltonians

The topological insulators that are easiest to describe are periodic crystalline insulators with two degrees of freedom (e.g. two orbitals) per [unit cell](/wiki/Unit_cell "Unit cell"). Although a lot of the following treatment is only true for those systems, it is easily generalizable to any crystalline topological insulator. Some notable examples of two-band systems are [graphene](/wiki/Graphene "Graphene") and the [Su-Shrieffer-Heeger model](/wiki/Su%E2%80%93Schrieffer%E2%80%93Heeger_model "Su–Schrieffer–Heeger model") (describing [polyacetylene](/wiki/Polyacetylene "Polyacetylene")). By separating the degrees of freedom from inside and outside the cell, one can write the wavefunction as

--- SOURCE 5: Getting a look under the hood of topological insulators ---
URL: https://www.anl.gov/article/getting-a-look-under-the-hood-of-topological-insulators

SUMMARY:
A certain kind of material, called a topological insulator, acts partially like one and partially like the other ― it behaves like a conductor at its surface



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://s2.studylib.net/store/data/005777281_1-c485fa1b58ba12928521911c39a10eb5-768x994.png
DESCRIPTION: The image illustrates the unique properties of topological insulators, showing conductance channels with spin polarization and a visual representation of their electronic surface states, alongside references to research from KITPC 2010 by Shoucheng Zhang at Stanford University.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://www.ijraset.com/images/text_version_uploads/imag+1_49450.png
DESCRIPTION: None

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://www.modern-physics.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/topological_insulators_in_advanced_materials.png
DESCRIPTION: The image explains the unique properties of topological insulators, emphasizing their conductive surfaces and insulating interiors, their robustness against impurities, and their applications in electronics and quantum computing.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://image1.slideserve.com/3253174/slide22-l.jpg
DESCRIPTION: Diagram shows the electronic band structure and surface states of a topological insulator, highlighting features like the Dirac cone, spin-momentum locking, and the effects of bulk and surface properties.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://image1.slideserve.com/2923180/what-are-topological-insulators-n.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image illustrates the unique electronic properties of topological insulators, showing their spin-orbit interaction, edge conduction, and immune surface states that are protected from scattering and defects.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Topological insulators (TIs) feature insulating interiors while exhibiting conductive, spin-polarized surface states, protected against impurities, enabling robust electronic behavior.

enriched_points.point_title: Significance in Spintronics and Quantum Computing
enriched_points.expanded_content: TIs are crucial for spintronics due to their spin-momentum locking, facilitating the development of efficient spin-based memory devices and quantum computing applications.
Ongoing research aims to explore new TIs and their properties, enhancing our understanding and paving the way for advancements in quantum technologies and materials science.
